title: Hydrodynamic Cooperons in Electron Fluids: Schwinger--Keldysh Derivation and Quantum Corrections to Magnetoresistance
abstract: We develop a Schwinger--Keldysh effective theory for quantum-interference corrections in a two-dimensional electron system in the hydrodynamic regime. Starting from the clean hydrodynamic fixed point, we introduce a minimal random-friction disorder model that generates a finite momentum-relaxation time within the self-consistent Born approximation. The disorder-averaged theory then allows us to construct a hydrodynamic Cooperon and to compute the associated self-energy corrections to the collective modes. Conservation laws protect the density and momentum sectors, so that the leading quantum-coherence correction is forced into the spin-two stress sector. The associated stress self-energy renormalizes the shear viscosity and modifies both the Gurzhi response and its low-field magnetohydrodynamic signatures.
summary: The paper derives a theory for quantum interference effects in electron fluids, specifically identifying a 'hydrodynamic Cooperon' that modifies stress relaxation. This mechanism predicts unique signatures in the magnetoresistance and Hall viscosity of high-mobility 2D electron gases, distinguishing quantum corrections from classical magnetohydrodynamic effects.
main_problem: Developing a consistent Schwinger-Keldysh effective theory for quantum-interference corrections (hydrodynamic Cooperons) in the hydrodynamic regime of 2D electron systems and determining their impact on transport properties.
main_result: The theory shows that the hydrodynamic Cooperon renormalizes the spin-two stress sector, leading to a reduction in effective shear viscosity and a characteristic low-field magnetoresistance dip/shoulder.
method: Real-time Schwinger-Keldysh field theory combined with a diagrammatic ladder resummation (Bethe-Salpeter equation) and the self-consistent Born approximation.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional electron system in the hydrodynamic regime (high-mobility conductors) subject to a minimal random-friction disorder model that introduces momentum relaxation.
key_observables: Shear viscosity, longitudinal and Hall viscosities, channel magnetoresistance, and stress relaxation rate.
important_parameters: Momentum-relaxation time (tau_mc), cyclotron frequency (omega_c), shear viscosity (nu), and the hydrodynamic ultraviolet cutoff (Lambda).
main_assumptions: Weak-damping/weak-relaxation limit, truncation of the angular harmonic hierarchy to m=0, +/-1, and +/-2, and the use of a sharp hydrodynamic momentum cutoff.
figures_summary: Figure 1 depicts the harmonic hierarchy of modes; Figure 4 shows the predicted low-field magnetoresistance dip and nonmonotonic temperature dependence; Figure 5 illustrates the Hall-viscosity kink and its temperature dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the hydrodynamic problem, develops the Schwinger-Keldysh framework with a random-friction disorder model, derives the Cooperon pole and its renormalization of the stress sector, analyzes the effect of a magnetic field (orbital dephasing and viscosity modification), and concludes by identifying experimental signatures in magnetoresistance and Hall viscosity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Hydrodynamic harmonic hierarchy and stress-sector closure. The density mode m = 0 and momentum modes m = ±1 are coupled by gradients and are protected by conservation laws, while the first nonconserved sector (represented by the dashed boxes) is the spin-two stress mode m = ±2. In the bare hydrodynamic closure, integrating out the stress sector generates the viscosity ν0. In the quantum-corrected theory, the hydrodynamic Cooperon renormalizes the stress relaxation rate, Γπ →Γπ + δΓ2, with Γπ the bare spin-two relaxation rate, which feeds back into the momentum sector as an effective viscosity νeff.

FIG. 2: Schematic illustration of the random-friction model. (a) In the clean hydrodynamic regime, a uniform driving field E produces a smooth electron-fluid velocity profile u(r) in a channel with no-slip boundaries. (b) In the presence of static inhomogeneous random friction, described by a spatially varying field ζ(r), the local drag force fdrag(r) = −ζ(r) u(r) opposes the flow and distorts the velocity streamlines. The color map represents the spatial variation of the friction strength, with stronger-friction regions producing a larger local suppression of the flow. This disorder realization provides the minimal hydrodynamic mechanism used in the Schwinger–Keldysh treatment to generate...

FIG. 3: Diagrammatic Bethe–Salpeter equation for the transverse hydrodynamic Cooperon C⊥(Ω, Q). The gray boxes denote the full Cooperon, the dashed vertical line is the random-friction disorder correlator ∆, and Γ0(∆) is the bare rung. The bubble ΠRA(Ω, Q) is built from one retarded and one advanced transverse hydrodynamic propagator. The second term contains one explicit disorder insertion; the Cooperon box on the right represents the remaining resummed ladder.

FIG. 4: Representative magnetoviscous response including the hydrodynamic-Cooperon stress correction. (a) Channel magnetoresistance ρch(B) at fixed temperature. The dashed black curve is the semiclassical result obtained from the bare stress relaxation rate Γπ(T), while the blue curve includes the Cooperon-induced correction Γπ →Γπ + δΓ2(T, B). The quantum-corrected curve (solid blue line) develops a narrow low-field dip because the Cooperon correction is largest at B = 0 and is suppressed by orbital magnetic dephasing at small finite field, while the broader background is controlled by the semiclassical magnetoviscous response (black dashed–dotted curve). (b) Fixed-field temperature cuts...

FIG. 5: Quantum correction to the Hall-viscous component of the magnetoviscosity tensor. (a) Semiclassical (black dashed curve) and quantum-corrected (blue solid curve) Hall viscosities as a function of magnetic field. The Cooperon correction preserves the odd-in-B symmetry of νxy, but suppresses the low-field slope through Γπ →Γπ + δΓ2(B, T). (b) Temperature dependence of νxy at two representative values of the magnetic field, blue B = 0.03T and orange B = 0.12T. In both cases the strong reduction of the quantum-corrected Hall viscosity with increasing temperature reflects the thermal dephasing effect of the hydrodynamic Cooperon in presence of magnetic fields.
